title: What does India actually make, and why does it matter?
teachingGoal: Show that an economy has a shape, not just a size, and that India's shape changed in an unusual way: it moved off the farm but largely skipped the factory and jumped to services. The reader should be able to read the three sector shares, see the stacked-bar composition, understand why manufacturing's share barely moved, why that pattern is unusual against the classic development path, and why it matters for jobs and for the kind of country India becomes.
sectionArc: What does India actually make? (the three sectors, the present-day composition, shown as a stacked bar)
How did the mix change since 1950? (agriculture's collapse as a share, the rise of services, manufacturing standing still)
Why did India skip the factory stage? (the classic farm-to-factory-to-services path; how India leapt to services; premature deindustrialisation)
Why does a thin factory floor matter? (manufacturing as a mass-employer; the output-versus-jobs gap, flagged honestly since the jobs share is not in this evidence)
What does this mean for India's future? (Make in India and the stakes, without over-claiming)
mustTeach: output divides into three broad sectors: agriculture, industry (including manufacturing), services
India's composition shifted hard: agriculture's share of output fell while services' share rose
manufacturing's share of output barely moved in 75 years, which is the unusual part
the classic development path runs farm to factory to services; India largely skipped the factory stage
a sector can shrink as a share of output while still employing a large share of people (flag the output-vs-jobs gap; do not state the jobs number, which is not in this evidence)

contextCards.fact: Economists have long observed a typical path of development: as a country grows richer, workers and output move first from agriculture into manufacturing, and only later into services. Britain, Germany, Japan, South Korea and China all industrialised through a manufacturing-led stage that absorbed millions of farm workers into factory jobs.
contextCards.source: Standard structural-transformation literature (Fisher-Clark three-sector model; widely established)

contextCards.fact: India is an unusual case in that it moved heavily into services, especially IT and business services after the 1990s, without first building a large manufacturing base. Economists describe this as services-led growth or 'premature deindustrialisation', and it is the backdrop to industrial-policy drives such as Make in India, launched in 2014.
contextCards.source: Standard Indian development-economics literature; 'premature deindustrialisation' (widely discussed); Make in India (Govt of India, 2014)
